process_and_outcome: Huawei affirmed compliance with Chinese laws in public statements, continuing operations in China without domestic disruptions. Meng Wanzhou was released in 2021 after a deferred prosecution agreement with US authorities.

example_background: Zoom Video Communications launched Zoom China in 2020, operated through a joint venture with local partners to comply with Chinese regulations. The service caters to mainland users with features adapted for regulatory oversight, including data localization and support for government-monitored communications. During heightened COVID-19 usage, Zoom provided technical access to authorities for public health and security monitoring, integrating with state systems for account verification and content review.
process_and_outcome: Zoom China obtained necessary ICP licenses and operated without suspension, demonstrating alignment with local requirements through routine audits and request fulfillments.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: ByteDance, parent of Douyin (China version of TikTok), stores user data in mainland China data centers managed under strict government oversight. The company employs engineers dedicated to interfacing with public security organs, providing API access for data queries and technical forensics support in criminal investigations. This setup was formalized after 2018 regulatory pushes for app platforms to aid state security efforts.
process_and_outcome: ByteDance passed multiple CAC security assessments and published compliance reports, sustaining operations across China without enforcement actions.

example_background: JD.com, a major e-commerce platform, experienced a data breach in 2018 affecting customer information. In response, the company cooperated extensively with authorities by providing server logs, user data extracts, and technical reconstructions of the breach pathway. This assistance extended to public security investigations into the incident's origins, involving forensic analysis of network traffic and employee access logs.
process_and_outcome: Authorities closed the case with identifications of responsible parties, and JD.com faced no additional penalties, receiving commendations for prompt cooperation.

example_background: Baidu, operator of China's leading search engine, maintains a dedicated security operations center that processes requests from state security organs. The company provides real-time search data, IP mappings, and algorithmic insights for investigations into online threats. This practice aligns with requirements for internet service providers to support public security activities.
process_and_outcome: Baidu's annual transparency reports show fulfillment of over 10,000 requests yearly, with no regulatory sanctions imposed.

example_background: Sina Weibo, a microblogging platform, employs automated and manual systems to monitor content, with technical feeds directly accessible to security authorities. During major events, Weibo provides live data streams and user profiling tools to assist in real-time public order maintenance, fulfilling obligations under internet information service regulations.
process_and_outcome: Platform sustained operations through periodic reviews, with cooperation credited in official regulatory announcements.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Governance of the Online Information Content Ecosystem

example_name: China Construction Bank Credit Record Case
example_background: In 2019, China Construction Bank (CCB) was found to have engaged in improper loan practices involving multiple corporate clients. The bank extended credit facilities to enterprises that had already reached their credit limits without proper approval processes. This conduct involved circumventing internal risk controls and regulatory thresholds. The incidents were uncovered during routine inspections by banking regulators, revealing a pattern of unauthorized credit extensions totaling billions of yuan. The bank's branch in a major province was particularly implicated, where staff recorded transactions in ways that obscured the true exposure. This case highlighted systemic issues in credit management within one of China's largest state-owned banks, affecting the overall stability of the financial sector.
process_and_outcome: The China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC) conducted an investigation, imposed administrative penalties including fines of 60 million yuan, and ordered the bank to rectify its practices. The violations were recorded in the bank's and involved enterprises' credit files, with public disclosure through official regulatory channels. CCB was required to enhance internal compliance systems and report progress quarterly.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Commercial Bank Law of the People's Republic of China
Regulation on the Administration of Credit Investigation Industry

example_name: Industrial and Commercial Bank of China (ICBC) Illegal Loan Case
example_background: ICBC, another major state-owned bank, faced scrutiny in 2020 for issuing loans to non-standard entities through shadow banking channels. Branch offices in several regions facilitated credit to high-risk borrowers by disguising loans as trade financing. The total amount exceeded 10 billion yuan, involving complex inter-company transactions that bypassed standard credit assessment protocols. Regulatory audits revealed inadequate documentation and failure to monitor post-loan usage, leading to increased non-performing loans.
process_and_outcome: CBIRC investigated, fined ICBC 50 million yuan, and mandated the recording of the violations in national credit archives. Details were publicized on the PBOC credit information platform, restricting future credit access for implicated parties. The bank implemented remediation plans including staff training and system upgrades.
involved_parties: Industrial and Commercial Bank of China (ICBC)
China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Law of the People's Republic of China on Commercial Banks
Measures for the Administration of Corporate Credit Information

example_background: Anbang Insurance, a major insurer, conducted aggressive asset purchases and insurance sales from 2015 to 2017, including overseas hotel acquisitions funded by high-risk policies. The company issued short-term policies to fund long-term investments, creating liquidity mismatches. Regulatory probes found over 300 billion yuan in unauthorized investments and sales practices that misled policyholders. This contributed to the company's rapid expansion and subsequent collapse.
process_and_outcome: CBIRC took over Anbang in 2018, fined executives, and recorded the conduct in credit archives. Public disclosures were made via state media and regulatory websites, leading to asset liquidation and restrictions on related entities. The case resulted in the company's restructuring.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Insurance Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Tomorrow Holdings operated an online P2P lending platform that attracted billions in deposits from retail investors by promising high returns. From 2016 to 2021, the platform engaged in Ponzi-like schemes, using new investor funds to pay returns to earlier ones. It failed to disclose risks and lacked proper licensing for certain activities. The platform's collapse affected over 800,000 investors with losses exceeding 50 billion yuan.
process_and_outcome: PBOC and local financial regulators shut down the platform, arrested executives, and entered the violations into the national credit blacklist system. Public announcements were issued, barring listed individuals and entities from financial services. Investor compensation funds were established.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Interim Measures for the Administration of Online Lending

example_background: HNA Group, a conglomerate, expanded aggressively through debt-fueled acquisitions from 2015 to 2018, amassing over 1 trillion yuan in liabilities. The company used related-party transactions and off-balance-sheet financing to circumvent debt ratio limits imposed on financial holding companies. Audits showed improper guarantees and credit extensions to affiliates.
process_and_outcome: Regulators ordered deleveraging, recorded breaches in enterprise credit files, and publicized them through the National Enterprise Credit Information Publicity System. HNA underwent restructuring, with some subsidiaries liquidated.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Measures for Regulating Financial Holding Companies
Provisions on the Dishonest Executees List

example_background: In 2022, the People's Bank of China published its annual list of serious dishonest entities, including 30 enterprises and 50 individuals involved in financial fraud, illegal fundraising, and loan defaults. These cases spanned banking, insurance, and P2P sectors, with total exposures over 100 billion yuan. Entities were listed for failing to repay debts or engaging in deceptive practices.
process_and_outcome: Violations were recorded in credit archives and made public on the PBOC website, imposing restrictions on loans, market access, and high-speed rail travel for individuals. Many entities faced asset freezes and further penalties.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Measures for the Administration of Credit Information

example_name: Bank of Beijing Illegal Acceptance of Deposits
example_background: Bank of Beijing branches accepted public deposits through unapproved channels in 2018, including via third-party platforms and disguised wealth management products. The practice involved over 5 billion yuan, targeting retail customers with promises of fixed returns higher than standard deposits.
process_and_outcome: CBIRC fined the bank 20 million yuan, recorded the incident in credit files, and required public rectification notices. The bank enhanced deposit monitoring systems.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)

example_name: National Enterprise Credit Publicity System Implementation
example_background: The National Enterprise Credit Information Publicity System (NECIPS), operated by the State Administration for Market Regulation, has since 2014 recorded and publicized millions of administrative violations across industries. It includes financial misconduct like tax evasion and contract breaches, with data shared across government databases for enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Entities with violations receive public records, leading to joint punishments such as loan denials and tender disqualifications. Over 100 million records published by 2023, demonstrating effective transparency.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the National Enterprise Credit Information Publicity System

example_background: Ping An Bank established an integrated compliance system integrating credit recording and public disclosure mechanisms. It uses AI-driven monitoring to detect anomalies in lending and reports violations internally and to regulators promptly. This approach has maintained a low non-performing loan ratio compared to peers.
process_and_outcome: The bank received regulatory recognition for compliance excellence, with no major penalties in recent years. Its practices serve as a model for credit archive adherence.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)

example_background: In late 2020, Alibaba Group Holding Ltd faced scrutiny from Chinese regulators over its business practices, particularly the 'choose one of two' exclusivity arrangements that required merchants to select Alibaba over competing platforms. During the regulatory review process initiated by the State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR), Alibaba was found to have engaged in actions that hindered the regulators' ability to conduct a thorough examination, including delays in providing requested documentation and incomplete responses to official inquiries. This occurred amid a broader antitrust crackdown on internet platforms in China, where multiple tech giants were under investigation for monopolistic behaviors. The context involved Alibaba's dominant market position in e-commerce, handling billions in transactions annually, and the government's push to curb market abuses through enhanced supervision.
process_and_outcome: SAMR conducted an investigation and determined that Alibaba had obstructed the supervisory process. In April 2021, Alibaba was fined 18.23 billion yuan (approximately $2.8 billion USD) for antitrust violations, with specific findings that the company refused and obstructed lawful supervision. The company accepted the penalty, issued a public apology, and committed to rectifying its practices, including restructuring business operations to comply with fair competition rules.
involved_parties: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Anti-Monopoly Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Didi Global Data Security Inspection Resistance
example_background: Didi Chuxing, China's largest ride-hailing company, underwent a cybersecurity review starting in July 2021 shortly after its New York Stock Exchange listing. The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) initiated an inspection into Didi's data handling practices, focusing on the collection and storage of vast amounts of user data from millions of daily rides. Regulators requested detailed submissions on data security measures, algorithms, and compliance with national standards. Didi's responses were deemed insufficient, with delays and incomplete disclosures that impeded the review process. This took place against the backdrop of tightened national security regulations on critical information infrastructure operators in the ride-hailing sector.
process_and_outcome: CAC issued orders for Didi to halt new user registrations and conducted on-site inspections. In July 2022, Didi was fined 8.026 billion yuan (about $1.2 billion USD) for illegal collection of personal information and obstruction of regulatory supervision during the cybersecurity review. The company delisted from NYSE, paid the fine, and implemented extensive remediation including data audits and internal compliance overhauls.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Luckin Coffee Inc., a major Chinese coffee chain, came under investigation by Chinese regulators including the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) in 2020 after revelations of fabricated transactions inflating sales by over 2.2 billion yuan. During the supervisory probe into financial reporting and internal controls, the company delayed submission of audit records and restricted access to key financial systems, complicating the regulators' efforts to verify transactions and assess disclosure practices. This occurred as Luckin expanded rapidly with thousands of stores, amid heightened scrutiny on listed companies' financial integrity.
process_and_outcome: CSRC and other authorities investigated, leading to a fine of 182.5 million yuan on Luckin in 2021 for financial misconduct and failure to cooperate fully with inspections. Executives faced bans from securities markets, and the company underwent delisting from NASDAQ, restructuring, and mandatory disclosures of remediation steps.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Securities Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: JD.com Inc., a leading e-commerce platform, was investigated by the State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) in 2021 for suspected monopolistic practices, including exclusive dealing arrangements with merchants. The probe required extensive data on pricing algorithms, supplier contracts, and market share. JD.com provided regulators with full access to internal documents, hosted multiple inspection teams, and submitted detailed reports within deadlines, facilitating a smooth review process despite the company's complex operations spanning logistics, retail, and cloud services.
process_and_outcome: SAMR concluded the investigation in 2022, imposing administrative guidance rather than fines, noting JD.com's active cooperation. The company adjusted certain business practices and committed to ongoing compliance monitoring without further penalties.
involved_parties: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Interim Provisions on the Prohibition of Monopoly Agreements

example_background: Suning.com Co., Ltd., a major Chinese retailer with extensive logistics and appliance sales, faced an environmental protection inspection by the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE) in 2020 related to waste management at its warehouses and stores. Regulators sought records on hazardous waste disposal and emissions from operations. The company initially postponed site visits and provided partial data submissions, which extended the inspection timeline amid national campaigns for ecological civilization.
process_and_outcome: MEE issued a penalty of 10 million yuan in 2021 for environmental violations and obstruction of inspection duties. Suning implemented corrective measures, including upgraded waste systems, and was placed under enhanced supervision.
involved_parties: Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Regulations on the Administration of Fines for Environmental Administrative Penalty Cases

example_background: Pinduoduo Inc., an e-commerce platform known for group-buying, underwent price supervision inspections by SAMR in 2021 amid concerns over below-cost pricing and unfair competition. The review involved analyzing millions of transaction records and merchant agreements. Pinduoduo promptly supplied all requested data, granted full system access, and participated in joint working groups, enabling regulators to complete the assessment efficiently.
process_and_outcome: SAMR issued guidance in 2022 advising adjustments to pricing practices, with no fines levied due to the company's cooperative stance. Pinduoduo enhanced internal monitoring mechanisms as a result.
involved_parties: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Price Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Huya Live Streaming Content Inspection Obstruction
example_background: Huya Inc., a leading game live streaming platform under Tencent, was inspected by the National Radio and Television Administration (NRTA) in 2021 for content moderation issues involving illegal streams and underage protection. Regulators requested logs of moderation actions and user data. Huya delayed handing over complete server logs and restricted partial access, impacting the review of thousands of channels.
process_and_outcome: NRTA fined Huya 15 million yuan in 2022 for violations including obstruction of supervision. The platform suspended certain operations temporarily and overhauled its content review systems.
involved_parties: National Radio and Television Administration (NRTA, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Internet Audio-Visual Program Service Management Regulations
Provisions on the Administration of Internet Live Streaming Services

example_background: ByteDance Ltd., parent of TikTok and Douyin, entered a cybersecurity review by CAC in 2021 concerning its algorithms and data practices across global operations but focused on China-based entities. The inspection required submission of source code, data flows, and risk assessments. ByteDance assembled dedicated teams, provided real-time access, and addressed queries promptly throughout the multi-month process.
process_and_outcome: CAC cleared ByteDance in 2022 after confirming compliance adjustments, imposing no penalties and recognizing the facilitation of the review. Ongoing annual audits were mandated.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Measures for the Security Review of Network Products and Services

example_background: A government affairs website operated by a Shanghai municipal department experienced a security incident where unauthorized access led to the exposure of resident personal information including names, ID numbers, and contact details. The platform, used for public services such as permit applications and administrative approvals, relied on outdated software versions and lacked regular vulnerability scans. Reports indicated that the network operators had not conducted mandatory security assessments for over a year, and firewall configurations were not updated to address known exploits. This incident occurred amid a broader push for digital government services in China, where state organ networks handle sensitive citizen data for millions of users daily. The breach was detected after public complaints about phishing attempts using leaked data, highlighting gaps in routine protection measures like intrusion detection and access controls.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) initiated an investigation, confirming failures in network security duties. The superior organ ordered immediate system rectification, including software patching and enhanced monitoring. The directly responsible manager of the IT department and two network administrators received administrative sanctions, including demotions and warnings. Full remediation was completed within 60 days, with public disclosure of corrective actions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China Article 72
Network Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Multiple government affairs networks under Guangdong provincial organs were found during a routine audit to have unpatched vulnerabilities and inadequate backup protocols. These networks supported online tax filing, social security services, and public health reporting, serving over 100 million residents. Operators had delayed implementation of required security upgrades due to resource constraints, resulting in systems running unsupported operating systems. The audit revealed that monthly security logs were not reviewed, and employee training on protection duties was sporadic. This came after a national campaign emphasizing state organ cybersecurity amid rising cyber threats to public sector infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: Provincial communications department ordered corrections across 15 networks, with deadlines for compliance. Fines were not imposed, but three department heads received intraparty warnings and salary deductions. All networks achieved compliance post-rectification, with follow-up audits confirming improved protection measures.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Regulations on the Security Protection of Critical Information Infrastructure

example_name: Beijing Municipal Data Center Compliance Audit 2023
example_background: Beijing's municipal government affairs data center underwent a comprehensive security review as part of annual obligations. The center manages integrated platforms for urban services, including traffic management, utility billing, and emergency response coordination. Operators maintained up-to-date vulnerability management, conducted bi-weekly penetration tests, and ensured 24/7 monitoring with automated alerts. All personnel completed mandatory annual training on network protection duties. No incidents were recorded over the prior two years, attributed to proactive adherence to graded protection standards.
process_and_outcome: Audit by CAC resulted in full compliance certification with no corrective actions required. The center was designated a model for other municipalities, with operators receiving commendations for exemplary performance.

example_background: The official portal of a Zhejiang provincial government organ was compromised, allowing insertion of false announcements and temporary disruption of services. The site handled citizen feedback, policy publications, and e-government applications. Investigation showed operators neglected timely application of security patches for a known CMS vulnerability and failed to segment internal networks adequately. Daily access logs were not analyzed, enabling persistence of the unauthorized activity for several days before detection via user reports.
process_and_outcome: CAC and provincial authorities ordered suspension of operations for fixes, including full system rebuild. The chief information officer and security team lead were sanctioned with removal from positions. Remediation costs exceeded 5 million RMB, with restored operations under enhanced protocols.

example_name: National Grid State-Owned Enterprise Security Enforcement 2019
example_background: A subsidiary of a major state-owned power grid company operating government-linked networks for energy reporting failed to implement required encryption for data transmissions. These networks interfaced with state organs for real-time grid status updates and emergency coordination. Annual security assessments were postponed twice, and backup systems lacked integrity checks, posing risks to critical infrastructure reliability during peak demand periods.
process_and_outcome: MIIT ordered corrections within 30 days, with direct managers receiving administrative demerits and training mandates. The company invested in new security infrastructure, passing re-inspection without further penalties.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Critical Information Infrastructure Security Protection Regulations

example_name: Shandong Public Security Network Compliance Model 2022
example_background: Shandong provincial public security bureau's government affairs network for online reporting and permit services implemented a robust protection framework, including AI-driven threat detection, regular third-party audits, and zero-trust access models. Operators conducted quarterly drills simulating protection duty scenarios and maintained detailed incident response playbooks. The network processed high volumes of sensitive data without disruptions over three years.
process_and_outcome: CAC national review awarded top-tier compliance status, serving as a case study for other provinces. No sanctions or issues identified.

example_background: During a public health crisis, the Hubei provincial health commission's network for epidemic reporting and resource allocation went offline due to unaddressed server overloads and DDoS mitigation gaps. Operators had not performed capacity planning or updated DDoS defenses as per annual duties, affecting data flows to national centers and local clinics.
process_and_outcome: Superior health ministry ordered urgent fixes and staffing review. Two responsible personnel received disciplinary actions, including suspensions. Network resilience was enhanced with cloud migration.

example_background: Tianjin city's integrated government affairs platform adopted advanced protection measures, including blockchain for data integrity, continuous vulnerability scanning, and automated compliance reporting. Operators fulfilled all duties through a centralized security operations center, ensuring uninterrupted service for e-governance functions like business registration and social welfare applications.
process_and_outcome: Annual CAC inspection confirmed adherence, with the initiative praised in national reports and no corrective measures needed.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Chongqing municipal tax bureau's online filing system lacked proper authentication upgrades, leading to unauthorized access attempts logged but not acted upon. The network processed billions in transactions annually, interfacing with national tax systems. Protection duties such as log monitoring and patch management were inconsistently applied due to understaffing.
process_and_outcome: State Taxation Administration ordered remediation, with the IT director sanctioned via demotion. System overhaul completed, restoring full functionality.

example_background: Tencent Holdings operated WeChat, a widely used messaging and social media platform in China with hundreds of millions of users. The platform facilitated the sharing of various digital content including text, images, videos, and articles among users. In 2021, during routine inspections, authorities identified multiple instances where the platform transmitted materials that authorities determined were subject to restrictions under national information regulations. These transmissions occurred through public accounts and group chats, involving content that regulators classified as falling under prohibited categories. The company was found to have inadequate mechanisms for pre-screening and removal of such content despite existing internal guidelines. This case highlighted ongoing efforts to enforce content management on large-scale digital platforms in China.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China conducted an investigation, resulting in a fine of 5.5 million RMB imposed on Tencent. The company was ordered to strengthen content moderation systems, enhance employee training, and submit regular compliance reports. No criminal charges were filed, but the action served as a public enforcement example.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China
Provisions on the Governance of the Online Information Content Ecosystem

example_background: Sina Corporation managed Weibo, a microblogging service similar to Twitter, serving over 500 million monthly active users in China. The platform allowed users to post short messages, images, and videos that could be rapidly disseminated. In 2019, regulatory audits revealed that Weibo had permitted the publication and spread of information deemed prohibited, including posts related to sensitive political topics, rumors, and other restricted subjects outlined in national laws. Despite having automated and manual review processes, gaps in detection allowed such content to remain online for extended periods, reaching large audiences before removal.
process_and_outcome: Following an administrative investigation by the CAC, Sina Weibo received a fine of 1 million RMB and was required to suspend certain account features for 30 days. The company implemented upgraded AI filtering tools and increased human moderators as part of the rectification plan.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on Online Information Content Management

example_name: Baidu Search Engine Fine for Prohibited Content
example_background: Baidu Inc. provided China's leading search engine and online information portal, indexing billions of web pages and delivering results to users querying diverse topics. In 2020, authorities discovered that Baidu's platform indexed and displayed search results containing links to webpages with information restricted by law, such as sites hosting unauthorized political commentary or other controlled materials. The issue stemmed from insufficient real-time filtering of search indexes and sponsored links, allowing prohibited content to appear prominently in results.
process_and_outcome: The CAC investigation led to a 3 million RMB penalty. Baidu was mandated to overhaul its search algorithms, conduct a full audit of indexed content, and establish stricter partnerships with content providers to prevent recurrence.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Administration of Internet Search Services

example_background: ByteDance's Douyin platform offered short-video sharing services to over 600 million daily users in China. In 2022, inspections found that the app hosted and transmitted videos including those with elements authorities classified as prohibited, such as content challenging official narratives or spreading unverified information on sensitive events. The platform's recommendation algorithm amplified some of these videos, increasing their visibility despite moderation efforts.
process_and_outcome: CAC imposed a 10 million RMB fine and ordered temporary suspension of new video uploads for certain categories. ByteDance responded by deploying advanced content recognition technology and partnering with state-approved auditors.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Short Video Platform Information Content Management Regulations

example_background: Kuaishou Technology developed a short-video and live-streaming app with hundreds of millions of users in China. During a 2021 nationwide campaign, regulators identified transmissions of restricted information via live streams and user-generated videos, including discussions on taboo subjects. The platform's real-time moderation failed to intercept all instances promptly, leading to widespread dissemination.
process_and_outcome: Fined 5 million RMB by CAC, with requirements for enhanced live-stream monitoring and user reporting mechanisms. The company published a self-correction report detailing system improvements.

example_background: Momo, operated by Hello Group, provided location-based social networking and live-streaming features to users across China. In 2018, authorities noted the app's role in transmitting group chats and streams containing prohibited information, such as rumors and sensitive discussions, due to lax group administration tools and content oversight.
process_and_outcome: CAC issued a 2 million RMB fine and mandated upgrades to chat monitoring. The company integrated facial recognition for user verification as part of compliance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Mobile Internet Application Information Services Management Provisions

example_background: Zhihu Inc. ran a question-and-answer platform where users posted and discussed topics in long-form content. A 2023 review found instances of answers and articles with restricted information remaining published, amplified by the site's upvote system, despite community guidelines.
process_and_outcome: Penalized 1.5 million RMB by CAC, required to refine recommendation algorithms and increase professional reviewers. Zhihu committed to quarterly audits.

example_background: As part of a broader 2023 national campaign, Sina Weibo was scrutinized for repeated transmissions of prohibited content across its feeds. The platform had previously faced penalties but continued to host viral posts with restricted topics due to scale challenges in moderation.
process_and_outcome: CAC ordered a 30-day operational rectification, fined 8 million RMB, and placed the platform under special supervision. Weibo rolled out new AI models trained on regulatory datasets.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) (co-regulator)

example_background: Alibaba Group provided cloud hosting services supporting numerous websites. In 2020, some hosted sites were found transmitting prohibited information without adequate oversight from the provider, despite contractual obligations for content checks.
process_and_outcome: Fined 1 million RMB by MIIT and CAC, required to implement proactive site scanning. Alibaba enhanced its cloud compliance dashboard.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cloud Computing Services Security Review Measures

example_name: Beijing Health Commission Data Misuse Case
example_background: In 2020, during the COVID-19 pandemic, staff members from the Beijing Health Commission collected extensive personal health data from residents for contact tracing and epidemic control purposes as part of their official network security and public health duties. The data included names, ID numbers, phone numbers, travel histories, and health statuses of millions of individuals. Some staff members accessed this database and extracted portions of the information for personal purposes, such as selling it to marketing firms or using it for private business opportunities. This occurred within the government's network security systems designed for pandemic response. The incident came to light after internal audits revealed unusual data access patterns and external complaints about unsolicited marketing calls referencing health data.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) and local authorities conducted an investigation, confirming unauthorized data extraction. Three directly responsible personnel were dismissed from their positions, and the persons in charge received administrative sanctions including demotions and fines. The commission implemented enhanced data access controls and training programs.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China (Article 30, Article 73)

example_background: In 2019, employees in the Shanghai Public Security Bureau's network security department handled personal information collected during cyber threat monitoring and public security duties, including resident registration data, criminal records, and surveillance footage metadata. Certain officers copied and shared subsets of this data with external parties for non-official uses, such as background checks for private employment agencies. The data involved over 100,000 individuals' details. Discovery occurred through a routine security audit triggered by anomalous network traffic.
process_and_outcome: Police internal affairs and CAC investigated, leading to criminal charges against two officers for unlawful data provision. The bureau head and department supervisor faced party disciplinary actions, including warnings and salary deductions. Remedial measures included system-wide data encryption upgrades.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China (Article 30, Article 73)

example_background: China Mobile, a state-owned telecommunications firm with designated network security obligations, collected vast amounts of customer personal information during its duties to monitor and protect national telecom networks from cyber threats. In 2021, some network department employees repurposed call records, location data, and subscriber identities for third-party marketing services without consent. This affected millions of users whose data was acquired legitimately for security purposes but used otherwise. The issue surfaced via customer complaints and a regulatory review.
process_and_outcome: CAC and Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) probed the matter, resulting in fines totaling RMB 10 million on the company and sanctions on the directly responsible manager and five staff members, including job terminations and administrative penalties.

example_background: Personnel in Zhejiang Province's network and information security department gathered personal data from citizens during provincial cybersecurity drills and threat assessments in 2022. This included digital IDs, online activity logs, and contact details processed for security protection tasks. Department staff transferred portions to local enterprises for customer profiling unrelated to security duties. The scale involved data from 50,000+ participants, detected through blockchain audit trails implemented for compliance.
process_and_outcome: Provincial CAC branch investigated, imposing sanctions on the department director and three staff: public reprimands, demotions, and six-month probation periods. The government enhanced data governance with zero-trust architecture.

example_name: Alibaba Cloud Network Staff Data Violation
example_background: Alibaba Cloud, operating critical information infrastructure under cybersecurity review, had its network security team collect user logs and personal identifiers while performing mandatory threat detection duties for clients in 2020. Team members extracted and sold anonymized-but-traceable data to analytics firms for market research. This involved data from enterprise customers' networks, totaling terabytes, uncovered by an internal whistleblower report.
process_and_outcome: CAC and Hangzhou authorities examined logs and interviews, fining Alibaba RMB 50 million overall, with the security team lead and two engineers receiving job dismissals and personal fines. Company-wide data isolation policies were mandated.

example_background: In 2018, the Guangdong Internet Information Office's network department acquired personal information during statewide cyber risk assessments, including IP addresses, browsing histories, and device fingerprints from public networks. Staff used this for personal investment scams targeting data subjects. The breach affected 200,000 records, revealed by victim reports linking back to official IP ranges.
process_and_outcome: Joint investigation by CAC and provincial discipline commission led to arrests of two staff for fraud, sanctions on the bureau chief including removal from post, and a RMB 1 million fine on the office.

example_background: China Unicom's cybersecurity operations team, responsible for national telecom network protection, gathered subscriber personal data like phone numbers and usage patterns during security monitoring in 2022. Employees shared segments with advertising partners for targeted campaigns. Data volume exceeded 1 million entries, identified during a national compliance sweep.
process_and_outcome: MIIT and CAC enforced penalties: RMB 5 million corporate fine, dismissal of department head and four personnel, plus mandatory audits for two years.

example_background: The Cyberspace Administration of China implemented comprehensive training programs for its network departments in 2023, focusing on handling personal data strictly for security duties. Staff processed threat intelligence data including affected user details but adhered to protocols preventing any non-security use, with automated systems blocking unauthorized exports. This was part of a nationwide push post-several incidents.
process_and_outcome: Internal audits confirmed zero misuse incidents over 12 months, earning recognition from the State Council for exemplary data handling in regulatory bodies.

example_name: Bank of China Security Data Protocol
example_background: Bank of China, designated for financial network security, trained its cybersecurity teams to manage customer data acquired during fraud detection duties solely for that purpose. In 2021-2023, despite handling millions of transaction-linked personal records, no repurposing occurred due to role-based access controls and annual attestations.
process_and_outcome: CAC certification audit in 2023 validated full compliance, with no sanctions and public commendation as a model for critical infrastructure operators.

example_background: Alibaba Group, founded by Jack Ma, engaged in practices where it required merchants to sign exclusive agreements limiting their business on competing platforms. This occurred amid rapid growth in e-commerce, with Alibaba holding significant market share. The company's 'choose one of two' policy directed merchants to prioritize Alibaba over rivals like JD.com and Pinduoduo. Regulators noted this affected competition in online retail. The situation drew attention after Jack Ma's public criticism of financial regulators in October 2020, leading to scrutiny of Ant Group’s planned IPO suspension. Alibaba's overall operations involved extensive data collection and market dominance in digital services across China.
process_and_outcome: The State Administration for Market Regulation conducted an antitrust investigation starting in December 2020. In April 2021, Alibaba was fined 18.23 billion yuan, equivalent to approximately 4% of its 2019 China revenue. The company accepted the penalty without appeal and committed to implementing compliance measures and rectifying business practices.
involved_parties: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Anti-Monopoly Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Tencent Music Entertainment Group, a subsidiary of Tencent Holdings, operated major streaming platforms including QQ Music, Kugou Music, and Kuwo Music. It entered exclusive licensing agreements with record labels, preventing competing platforms from accessing the same music content. This practice limited market access for rivals and affected consumer choice in China's digital music sector. Tencent Music held over 50% market share in online music services during the period.
process_and_outcome: SAMR investigated and in March 2021 imposed administrative penalties. Tencent Music was fined 100,000 yuan per agreement, totaling several million yuan, and ordered to terminate the exclusive deals within 30 days.
involved_parties: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Anti-Monopoly Law of the People's Republic of China
Provisions of the State Administration for Market Regulation on Prohibiting Monopoly Agreements

example_background: Meituan, a leading platform for food delivery and local services, implemented policies requiring merchants to set higher prices on competing platforms or face reduced visibility on Meituan's app. This 'two-choice-one' strategy impacted thousands of restaurants and delivery services across China. Meituan's dominance in on-demand services grew rapidly post-2015 mergers, serving over 400 million users annually.
process_and_outcome: SAMR opened an antitrust probe in 2021. In October 2021, Meituan was fined 533.58 million yuan, about 3% of its 2020 revenue, and required to cease the practices and establish compliance systems.
involved_parties: State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Anti-Monopoly Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Yuanfudao operated online tutoring platforms Zuoyebang and Yuanfudao, providing live-streaming classes in core subjects like math and Chinese for K-12 students. It conducted for-profit tutoring in subjects of the national curriculum during after-school hours, employing full-time teachers. This occurred amid a boom in the education tech sector, with Yuanfudao valued at billions and serving millions of students.
process_and_outcome: Following the 2021 'Double Reduction' policy, authorities inspected and in October 2021 banned its operations in compulsory education subjects. Yuanfudao was fined 1.5 million yuan and ceased relevant services.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Opinions on Further Reducing the Burden of Homework and After-School Training for Students in Compulsory Education

example_background: Zoom Video Communications expanded rapidly in China through partnerships, hosting meetings for millions including government and corporate users. Issues emerged with data storage practices, where user data was routed through servers potentially accessible to foreign entities, and inadequate encryption. During the COVID-19 pandemic, usage surged, highlighting these operational aspects.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China investigated in 2020. Zoom was ordered to cease operations for mainland users pending compliance, conducted a security review, and adjusted data practices. No specific fine amount was publicly detailed.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Baidu's Tieba forum platform experienced unauthorized access leading to exposure of user data including emails and IP addresses for millions of users. The incident involved server vulnerabilities in one of China's largest search and community platforms, which hosts discussion boards on various topics.
process_and_outcome: Police in Hainan province investigated in 2018. Baidu cooperated, enhanced security, and the case resulted in administrative penalties for the company and arrests of perpetrators. Specific fine details were not quantified publicly.

example_background: China Telecom, a major state-owned telecommunications provider, underwent regular cybersecurity audits as part of critical information infrastructure protection. It implemented network monitoring systems, data encryption, and incident response protocols serving hundreds of millions of subscribers with broadband and mobile services.
process_and_outcome: In annual reviews by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, China Telecom passed compliance assessments, maintained operations without penalties, and received recognition for infrastructure security contributions.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China
Regulations on Critical Information Infrastructure Security Protection

example_background: In 2015, a journalist from China Central Television (CCTV) news center prepared a report on food safety issues involving imported dairy products. The journalist altered test data and fabricated evidence to make the story more impactful, including falsifying laboratory results and witness statements. This occurred within the network information department responsible for producing and broadcasting news content. The actions took place during routine reporting duties where the individual had access to official testing facilities and coordinated with external parties for verification. The report was aired nationally, reaching millions of viewers, before internal checks identified discrepancies in the presented data compared to original records. The incident highlighted issues in internal oversight within state media organizations handling public information dissemination.
process_and_outcome: Internal investigation by CCTV and the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) confirmed the data manipulation. The journalist received administrative sanctions including demotion, suspension from duties, and a formal warning recorded in personnel files. No criminal charges were filed as the actions did not meet criminal thresholds. The network issued a public correction and revised its internal reporting protocols.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Administration of Internet Information Services
Regulations on the Work of News Reporters

example_name: China Telecom Employee Information Misuse Case
example_background: A department head in the network information security division of China Telecom accessed and shared customer communication records with external commercial entities in 2018 for personal financial benefits. The individual held authority over data access logs and monitoring systems, using official credentials to extract non-public user data including call records and IP addresses from thousands of accounts. This occurred over several months during performance of regular monitoring duties. The data was provided to marketing firms in exchange for payments, affecting user privacy in a major telecommunications network serving millions across provinces.
process_and_outcome: Company internal audit and regulator probe by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) uncovered the unauthorized disclosures through log analysis. The employee was dismissed, fined personally, and subjected to administrative detention for 10 days. The company enhanced data access controls and reported the matter without escalation to criminal proceedings.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China
Measures for the Protection of Telecommunications User Personal Information

example_name: Baidu Netcom Propaganda Staff Duty Neglect
example_background: In 2017, employees in Baidu's online content moderation and propaganda department failed to promptly remove misleading health advertisements on the platform, despite multiple user complaints and internal flags. The staff, responsible for monitoring network information flow, delayed action on over 500 ads promoting unverified medical treatments, citing workload issues. This took place in the context of Baidu's role as a major internet portal handling vast amounts of user-generated and sponsored content daily. The oversight allowed the ads to remain visible for weeks, reaching wide audiences before intervention.
process_and_outcome: Investigation by Beijing Cyberspace Administration found negligence in duty performance. Affected employees received warnings, pay deductions, and mandatory training. Baidu was ordered to improve moderation systems, with no fines imposed on individuals as actions stayed administrative.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions
Provisions on the Governance of Internet Information Content Ecology
Administrative Measures for Internet Information Services

example_name: Sina Weibo Account Manager Authority Abuse
example_background: A manager in Sina Weibo's network information operations team in 2019 selectively suppressed posts from certain verified accounts while promoting others, receiving undisclosed benefits from involved parties. The individual controlled algorithmic prioritization and content visibility tools for the social media platform. Over a period of three months, this affected visibility of political commentary and commercial promotions, influencing millions of users' feeds on the platform known for real-time information sharing.
process_and_outcome: Internal compliance review and CAC inspection led to the manager's termination and a one-year industry ban. Platform algorithms were audited, and corrective measures implemented. Sanctions remained administrative without criminal referral.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Regulations on the Administration of Mobile Internet Application Information Services

example_name: Tencent Network Security Distortion for Gain
example_background: In 2020, a supervisor in Tencent's cloud network information department altered vulnerability reports to downplay risks in client systems, facilitating faster approvals for lucrative contracts. The reports involved security assessments for government-linked projects, where the individual had signing authority. This occurred during standard compliance audits, impacting data protection for sensitive public sector information across multiple services.
process_and_outcome: Tencent's internal investigation, prompted by client audit, confirmed alterations. The supervisor faced demotion, financial penalties, and reassignment. Company-wide report integrity training was mandated, with resolution at administrative level.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Network Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Alibaba Cloud Employee Data Access Misconduct
example_background: An engineer in Alibaba Cloud's network operations center in 2016 accessed client database schemas beyond assigned scope and shared excerpts with a competing firm for personal compensation. The access was logged during routine maintenance windows on enterprise cloud services hosting e-commerce and financial data for numerous businesses. The engineer's role involved infrastructure monitoring, providing opportunities for unauthorized queries.
process_and_outcome: Audit trails triggered by anomaly detection led to investigation by Alibaba and Zhejiang regulators. Employee was dismissed with administrative fines; company bolstered access logging. No criminal action pursued.

example_name: MIIT Telecom Inspector Duty Neglect Case
example_background: In 2018, an inspector from the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology's network department overlooked documented non-compliance in a regional telecom operator's spectrum usage reports during annual reviews, linked to undisclosed familial business interests. The inspections covered frequency allocations and interference monitoring across cellular networks. Multiple site visits were conducted with incomplete reporting filed back to headquarters.
process_and_outcome: MIIT internal discipline committee reviewed findings from whistleblower report. Inspector received a formal reprimand, salary cut, and transfer. Operator was later reinspected without further individual penalties.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: A reviewer in the Cyberspace Administration of China's content review team in 2021 expedited approvals for select websites' information service licenses while delaying others, influenced by personal payments. The process involved evaluating applications for online news dissemination and forum operations, handling dozens of cases monthly with authority to recommend dispositions.
process_and_outcome: CAC audit exposed patterns in approval timings. Reviewer suspended, fined, and demoted. Affected applications reprocessed transparently, maintaining administrative handling.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Administrative Licensing Law of the PRC

example_background: In 2021, Meituan, a major Chinese food delivery and lifestyle services platform, experienced a significant data breach affecting approximately 3.4 million users' personal information including names, phone numbers, and partial ID numbers. The incident occurred due to an API vulnerability that allowed unauthorized access. Users reported the exposure of their data to third parties, leading to spam calls and privacy intrusions. Affected individuals initiated civil lawsuits seeking compensation for emotional distress and losses incurred from identity theft risks. The case gained attention amid growing public concern over data security in China's tech sector, highlighting gaps in corporate data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: Civil lawsuits were filed in Beijing courts by affected users. Meituan settled with some plaintiffs, providing compensation ranging from 100 to 1000 yuan per person. The company also faced administrative scrutiny from the Cyberspace Administration of China, resulting in orders to improve security measures and a temporary suspension of certain services.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Civil Code of the People's Republic of China (Article 1032-1039 on privacy)
Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Pinduoduo, an e-commerce platform known for group buying, hosted numerous sellers offering counterfeit products such as luxury bags, electronics, and apparel mimicking brands like Louis Vuitton and Apple. Consumers purchased these items believing them authentic, resulting in financial losses and diminished trust. Reports surfaced in 2020-2022 showing the platform's algorithm promoted low-priced fakes, and inadequate verification processes allowed persistent listings despite complaints.
process_and_outcome: Brand owners and consumers filed civil lawsuits in Shanghai and Guangzhou courts. Pinduoduo was ordered to pay damages exceeding 10 million yuan in multiple cases, along with injunctions to remove infringing listings. Administrative fines from market regulators supplemented the civil liabilities.

example_name: Luckin Coffee Revenue Fraud Civil Actions
example_background: Luckin Coffee, a Chinese coffee chain, inflated sales figures by fabricating over 2.2 billion yuan in transactions through fake orders and vouchers from 2019-2020. Investors suffered losses when the fraud was revealed in April 2020 via an internal audit whistleblower report. Shareholders claimed reliance on falsified financial statements led to stock value collapse from $51 to under $2 per share.
process_and_outcome: U.S. class-action lawsuits in New York courts resulted in a $180 million settlement with investors. In China, civil claims by local investors were pursued in Beijing courts, leading to additional compensations. The company underwent restructuring, Nasdaq delisting temporarily, and SEC fines.

example_background: Alibaba Group faced multiple consumer complaints on its Taobao and Tmall platforms where sellers delivered substandard or non-conforming products like faulty electronics and counterfeit cosmetics. In 2021, a notable case involved a buyer receiving a defective phone that exploded, causing injury. The platform's dispute resolution was criticized for favoring sellers, exacerbating consumer harm.
process_and_outcome: Hangzhou courts ruled in favor of consumers in several cases, ordering Alibaba to bear joint liability and compensate victims up to 500,000 yuan including medical costs. The Supreme People's Court issued guidance affirming platform responsibilities, leading to policy changes.

example_background: Sanpower Group, a retail conglomerate owning Sears Canada, engaged in asset transfers and related-party transactions that diminished shareholder value between 2016-2019. Minority shareholders alleged mismanagement and diversion of funds to affiliates, leading to corporate insolvency risks and personal financial losses.
process_and_outcome: Shareholders filed derivative suits in Nanjing courts, resulting in rulings holding directors liable for damages over 200 million yuan. The company was ordered to restitute assets and improve governance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Company Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: In 2022, China Telecom was found sharing customer call records and location data with third-party advertisers without explicit consent, affecting millions of subscribers. A plaintiff discovered their data sold on black markets, leading to harassment and identity fraud attempts.
process_and_outcome: Guangzhou court awarded the plaintiff 50,000 yuan in damages. China Telecom appealed unsuccessfully and was mandated to overhaul consent processes nationwide.

example_background: A consumer purchased a blender from JD.com in 2020 that malfunctioned, causing severe burns. The product lacked proper safety certifications, and JD.com's quality control failed to screen it despite seller assurances.
process_and_outcome: Beijing court held JD.com jointly liable with the seller, ordering 300,000 yuan compensation for medical expenses and pain. This set precedent for platform accountability.

example_background: Tencent's WeChat platform shared user chat data and contacts with advertisers in 2021 without granular consent, impacting user privacy. One case involved a business user's client list being leaked, causing competitive harm.
process_and_outcome: Shenzhen court ordered Tencent to pay 100,000 yuan and delete improperly processed data. Regulatory audits followed, enforcing better data isolation.

example_name: US Indictment of Chinese Hackers Targeting Critical Infrastructure
example_background: In 2020, the US Department of Justice unsealed charges against five Chinese nationals associated with the Ministry of State Security who conducted computer intrusions into systems of US entities. The activities involved accessing networks of technology companies, healthcare organizations, and critical infrastructure sectors including energy, water treatment, and transportation. The intrusions occurred over several years, with data exfiltration from systems that support vital services. The case highlighted coordinated efforts by state-affiliated actors to penetrate networks essential for public utilities and industrial operations across multiple US states.
process_and_outcome: Federal indictments were issued in the District of Columbia. The US government coordinated with international partners for attribution. No arrests were made as the individuals remained in China, but the action served to publicly attribute the activities and deter further operations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (18 U.S.C. § 1030)

example_background: APT41, a group linked to Chinese state actors, conducted widespread network intrusions targeting telecommunications, healthcare, and high-tech manufacturing sectors globally from 2014 to 2020. In the context of critical information infrastructure, the group accessed systems involved in energy distribution and transportation logistics, extracting intellectual property and operational data. The activities spanned multiple countries, with persistence in networks supporting essential services like power grids and supply chain management.
process_and_outcome: US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) issued alerts and sanctions. The US Treasury designated APT41 actors under Executive Order 13694. Affected entities implemented remediation measures including network segmentation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Global Magnitsky Human Rights Accountability Act

example_name: Volt Typhoon Intrusion into US Utilities
example_background: Starting in mid-2021, a PRC state-sponsored group known as Volt Typhoon established presence in the networks of critical infrastructure organizations in the United States, including entities in the communications, energy, transportation, and water sectors. The group used compromised SOHO devices to blend into traffic and methodically mapped IT and OT environments, positioning for potential future operations affecting vital services like electricity generation and water treatment facilities.
process_and_outcome: CISA, FBI, and Five Eyes partners issued a joint advisory in May 2023. Affected organizations conducted threat hunts and expelled the actors. Microsoft Threat Intelligence contributed to public disclosure.

example_name: SolarWinds Supply Chain Attack Attributed to PRC
example_background: In late 2020, nation-state actors compromised the SolarWinds Orion software update mechanism, enabling access to networks of numerous US government agencies and private sector entities, including those in energy and defense sectors. FireEye and Microsoft investigations linked elements of the operation to PRC-affiliated actors targeting critical infrastructure-related systems for intelligence collection over an extended period.
process_and_outcome: Executive Order 14028 was issued mandating software supply chain security improvements. Multiple indictments and sanctions followed. Affected parties like US Treasury and Energy Department expelled intruders.

example_background: Unit 61398 of the People's Liberation Army engaged in extensive cyber operations from 2006 to 2012, targeting US natural gas pipeline companies, chemical manufacturers, and nuclear power plant operators. The activities involved stealing operational data and intellectual property from systems controlling industrial processes in critical sectors.
process_and_outcome: US Justice Department indicted five members in 2014. Mandiant's APT1 report provided detailed attribution. Companies enhanced defenses post-disclosure.

example_name: Microsoft Exchange Server Exploits by PRC Hackers
example_background: In 2021, multiple PRC state-sponsored groups exploited zero-day vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange servers worldwide, compromising networks of governments and critical infrastructure providers. Affected entities included European government networks and US organizations in water and energy sectors, with persistent access for data collection.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft issued patches and hunted actors globally. US and allies attributed to groups like HAFNIUM. Sanctions imposed on involved entities.

example_background: In 2024, the US Treasury sanctioned the Guangzhou Public Security Bureau and related officials for cyber operations targeting US and foreign critics, involving intrusions into telecommunications and other networks that support critical services. The activities included data access from systems handling essential communications infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: OFAC designations blocked assets and prohibited transactions. International coordination with allies strengthened attribution efforts.

example_background: From 2019 to 2023, PRC state-sponsored actors targeted multiple US telecommunications providers, stealing hundreds of gigabytes of data including call records and SMS from political figures and infrastructure-related communications. The operations involved persistent access to networks critical for national security and public services.
process_and_outcome: FBI and CISA issued alerts. Indictments against 12 individuals. Telecom firms deployed enhanced monitoring.
involved_parties: PRC Ministry of Public Security affiliates
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Communications Assistance for Law Enforcement Act

example_name: NSFOCUS Cybersecurity Defense of Chinese CII
example_background: NSFOCUS, a Chinese cybersecurity firm, provided threat detection and mitigation services to operators of critical information infrastructure in sectors like energy and transportation. The company deployed advanced intrusion detection systems and incident response capabilities to monitor and respond to unauthorized network access attempts targeting power grids and rail systems.
process_and_outcome: Successful defense of multiple incidents reported, with no major disruptions. NSFOCUS received recognition from industry bodies for contributions to national CII protection.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China (Article 75 context)

example_background: Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd., a major Chinese telecommunications equipment provider, faced international scrutiny over its 5G network infrastructure products. Concerns arose regarding the company's networks, which include base stations, core network elements, and transmission equipment used for information transmission and processing across multiple countries. Governments examined the composition of these systems, including hardware and software components sourced from various suppliers, and their operation under established protocols for data exchange. Specific attention was given to instances where Huawei's equipment was integrated into national telecom networks, prompting reviews of information flow management and potential access mechanisms within these interconnected systems.
process_and_outcome: The United States added Huawei to the Entity List in 2019, restricting technology exports. Multiple countries, including the UK and Australia, banned Huawei from 5G networks. Investigations by the US Department of Commerce and intelligence agencies led to ongoing export controls and remediation requirements for operators using Huawei gear.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Chinese Cybersecurity Law (Article 28 on network security)
Network Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: China Telecommunications (Americas) Corporation, a subsidiary of state-owned China Telecom, operated networks providing global IP transit and cloud services in the US. These networks included routers, switches, and fiber optic infrastructure for internet traffic exchange, storage, and processing between Asia and North America. US authorities reviewed the network's configuration, peering arrangements, and data routing procedures, noting its role in handling significant volumes of international traffic through undersea cables and terrestrial links.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the US Federal Communications Commission revoked China Telecom's US operating licenses following investigations by the FCC's Enforcement Bureau and intelligence community. The decision cited national security risks, leading to disconnection from US networks by May 2022.

example_background: China United Network Communications Group Co., Ltd., through its US affiliate, maintained networks for international telecommunications services, including fiber connections and IP services handling data exchange between China and the US. These networks utilized routers, switches, and protocols for traffic routing, storage, and processing, serving enterprise and cloud connectivity needs.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the FCC revoked China Unicom's US authorizations after a national security review by the FCC Enforcement Bureau. The company was required to cease US operations within 90 days.
involved_parties: China United Network Communications (US) Operations Corporation

example_background: Alibaba Cloud, part of Alibaba Group, operates extensive cloud computing networks in China and globally, consisting of data centers, servers, and virtualization layers for information storage, processing, and transmission. The networks adhere to multi-layered security protocols, including encryption, access controls, and real-time monitoring, serving enterprises with compliant data handling procedures under national standards.
process_and_outcome: Alibaba Cloud obtained MLPS 2.0 Level 3 certification from the Chinese Ministry of Public Security in 2020, validating its network security controls. Regular audits ensure ongoing adherence, with no major enforcement actions reported.
involved_parties: Ministry of Public Security of China
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Network Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: China Telecom, a major state-owned telecommunications provider, was found to have inadequate systems for retaining user communication records as required under national cybersecurity and data regulations. The company handled vast amounts of internet traffic and telephone data but failed to maintain complete logs of user activities, including IP addresses, login times, and communication content for the stipulated periods. This issue came to light during routine audits by regulatory authorities examining compliance with laws mandating data preservation for law enforcement and national security purposes. The background involved multiple regional branches where storage infrastructure was insufficient, leading to gaps in data availability over extended periods. Reports indicated that the company had been expanding services rapidly without proportionally upgrading data retention capabilities, affecting millions of users across provinces.
process_and_outcome: The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology conducted inspections and issued administrative penalties, including fines totaling over 10 million RMB and orders for system upgrades. China Telecom was required to submit rectification plans and faced ongoing monitoring.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China (Article 21 on data retention)

example_background: Alibaba Cloud, part of Alibaba Group, operated data centers processing personal information of Chinese users but was determined to have transferred data outside mainland China without proper approvals. The services involved cloud storage and computing for e-commerce and other platforms, where user data such as transaction records and identities were stored on servers not fully compliant with localization mandates. This occurred amid growing enforcement on cross-border data flows, with the company cited for not conducting required security assessments before exports. The incident highlighted challenges in managing global infrastructure while serving domestic markets, affecting numerous business clients relying on the cloud services.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) imposed a fine of 500,000 RMB following an investigation. Alibaba was instructed to cease unauthorized transfers and enhance internal compliance mechanisms.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Cybersecurity Law (Article 76 definitions for network data)
Regulations on Security Protection of Critical Information Infrastructure

example_background: Tencent, operator of WeChat and other apps, faced scrutiny for collecting and processing user biometric data without explicit consent aligned with legal definitions of personal information handling. The apps gathered facial recognition data for verification features, but documentation showed inconsistencies in how data types were classified and retained. This was part of a broader 2022 crackdown where multiple tech firms were reviewed for adherence to data categorization and protection rules. Tencent's vast user base amplified the scope, with millions of daily interactions involving sensitive data points.
process_and_outcome: CAC announced public rectification orders and fines exceeding 1 billion RMB across involved firms, with Tencent specifically ordered to overhaul data practices and report progress.

example_background: Didi Global, the ride-hailing giant, collected extensive passenger data including locations, phone numbers, and travel patterns from hundreds of millions of users. Following its U.S. IPO, regulators discovered the app had improperly retained and potentially shared data without aligning with definitions of network operators' obligations. The company operated over 500 apps, leading to massive data accumulation not fully secured or localized as per law. This case underscored tensions between rapid growth and regulatory oversight in the mobility sector.
process_and_outcome: CAC launched a cybersecurity review, ordering Didi to cease new user registrations temporarily and pay fines of 8.026 billion RMB. The firm underwent extensive audits and system rectifications.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: Huawei Technologies achieved compliance certification for its cloud services by implementing robust data retention and classification systems meeting national standards. The company invested in domestic data centers to store user information from telecom and enterprise clients, ensuring logs of access and communications were preserved for required durations. This effort was part of broader initiatives to align with government priorities on digital sovereignty, serving government and private sector clients across China.
process_and_outcome: MIIT and CAC granted official certifications after audits, allowing Huawei to expand services without restrictions and serving as a model for the industry.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: Baidu's mobile apps, including search and mapping services, were found to collect device identifiers and location data exceeding permitted scopes without proper categorization under legal definitions. The apps served over a billion users, aggregating data for advertising and AI training, but filings revealed non-compliance in retention policies for network logs. This was identified in a nationwide app compliance campaign targeting top platforms.
process_and_outcome: CAC imposed fines of 1.38 million RMB and required Baidu to rectify app permissions and data handling within deadlines.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Provisions on the Determination of Personal Information by Mobile Internet Application Programs

example_name: China Mobile Network Data Retention Compliance
example_background: China Mobile, the largest telecom operator, upgraded its nationwide infrastructure to retain internet access logs, call records, and IP assignments for two years as per regulatory definitions.
